d-inheritance.content:
### What Screen Design Inherited From Print

The list is longer than most practitioners realise.

**The column** — derived from the manuscript tradition via the press sheet, which used columns to maximise typography per sheet.

**The baseline grid** — inherited from the ruled compositing stick. Metal type sat on a baseline; digital type mimics this structure.

**Leading** — named for the strips of lead alloy that printers placed between lines of type to control spacing.

**The typeface as system** — the idea that a family of related weights and styles constitutes a coherent typographic tool. Print culture produced this long before type designers worked digitally.

**The em** — a unit of measurement equal to the current point size, inherited intact from metal typesetting.

**Page margins** — originally a physical requirement (paper stretches; type cannot be set to the edge; binding eats space). Now a convention maintained because it works.

**The reading measure** — the recommendation that a comfortable text column is 45–75 characters wide, derived from eye movement research conducted on print readers in the 1920s and still broadly applicable.

Understanding these inheritances does not mean preserving them uncritically. But it means understanding why they are there — which is the precondition for knowing when to change them.
